serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Laal Maas is approximately 300 g (one bowl), containing roughly 550 kcal, 35 g protein, 40 g fat, and 15 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Mutton Curry / Meat Curry composite; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb/mutton with spices
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Laal Maas specifically. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (mutton curry composite, ~180–190 kcal/100g range) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for lamb stew/curry (NDB 17174 and related entries). The input values (183.33 kcal, 11.67 g protein, 13.33 g fat, 5.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g) are consistent with this composite and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 266.67 mg/100g is within the expected range for a salted curry preparation. Detailed fatty acid fractions (MUFA, PUFA) are not reported in ICMR-NIN for this composite and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283. — analogous estimate based on meat-based curry with low carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Laal Maas in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on analogous meat-based curry dishes with low carbohydrate content (~5 g/100g) and high protein/fat content, consistent with published GI values for meat curries in Atkinson et al. 2008 and Brand-Miller et al. peer-reviewed tables. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (30 × 15 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 4.5; per 100 g = (30 × 5.0) / 100 = 1.5; GL per serving (300 g) = 4.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 266.67 mg/100g combined with saturated fat 6.67 g/100g exceeding the 5 g/100g threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Laal Maas to small portions (≤150 g) to keep sodium intake below 400 mg per meal, and request reduced-ghee preparation to lower saturated fat, which contributes to arterial stiffness and elevated blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (5.0 g/100g) and high protein (11.67 g/100g) producing minimal postprandial glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Laal Maas has a low glycaemic impact due to its minimal carbohydrate content and high protein, which slows gastric emptying; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should pair it with low-GI carbohydrates such as brown rice or whole wheat roti and limit portion size to manage cumulative saturated fat intake, which can worsen insulin resistance over time.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.67 g/100g from ghee and red meat, trans fat 0.17 g/100g, and cholesterol 40 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Laal Maas only occasionally and in small portions (≤150 g), as the high saturated fat from ghee and red mutton raises LDL cholesterol, and the trans fat content further increases cardiovascular risk; requesting preparation with reduced ghee or substituting with a small amount of canola oil can partially mitigate this risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid (omega-6) from red mutton and saturated fat from ghee promoting pro-inflammatory cytokines
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit or avoid Laal Maas due to the high arachidonic acid content of red mutton amplifying TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways, and should monitor individual response to Mathania chillies (nightshade family) which may trigger symptom flares in sensitive patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Laal Maas (Hindi: लाल मांस, meaning 'red meat') is a fiery, slow-cooked mutton curry originating from Rajasthan, India, and is considered one of the most iconic dishes of Rajasthani royal cuisine. The dish derives its characteristic deep crimson colour and intense heat from Mathania dried red chillies, a variety grown in the Mathania region near Jodhpur, which are distinct from ordinary red chillies in their fruity, smoky heat profile. The mutton — typically bone-in pieces — is slow-cooked in a generous quantity of ghee (clarified butter) with whole spices including cloves, cardamom, bay leaves, and cumin, along with a paste of onion, garlic, and ginger. Yogurt is incorporated to tenderise the meat and add a subtle tang that balances the heat. The result is a rich, deeply spiced, oil-glistening curry with tender, fall-off-the-bone mutton in a thick, aromatic red gravy. Nutritionally, per 100 g Laal Maas provides approximately 183 kcal, 11.7 g protein, 13.3 g fat (of which 6.7 g is saturated fat from ghee), and only 5.0 g carbohydrate, making it a low-glycaemic dish. The high saturated fat content is a concern for cardiovascular disease and hypertension management. Sodium at 266.67 mg/100g is in the borderline range. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal. It is traditionally served with bajra roti (pearl millet flatbread) or steamed rice and is a centrepiece of festive and celebratory Rajasthani meals. In Singapore, it appears on Indian restaurant menus as a premium curry option.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Laal Maas?
Is Laal Maas good for high blood pressure?
What chilli is used in Laal Maas?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mutton curry composite and cross-validated with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy lamb/mutton stew entries. No single approved-source entry exists specifically for Laal Maas. Input values are consistent with the composite range and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for Laal Maas (Rajasthani mutton curry) are not individually reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this specific dish composite as of 2024. trans_fat_g value of 0.17 g/100g is retained from input; this is plausible for a ghee-containing preparation as ghee contains naturally occurring trans fatty acids (vaccenic acid). Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (30 × 15.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 4.5. The input source listed 'https://foodo.sg/' under HPB Singapore, which is a third-party aggregator and not an approved primary source; nutrient values have been re-anchored to ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central. Country of origin in input was listed as 'Singapore' which is incorrect for a Rajasthani dish; corrected to 'Rajasthan, India'. Ethnic tags 'Naan' and 'Biryani' in input are dish names, not ethnic tags, and have been removed. Core macros (protein_g 11.67, fat_g 13.33, carbohydrate_g 5.0) are all non-zero and consistent with a meat-based curry; no zero-macro justification required.
